nodes.label: A-1 - Blinded within-subject crossover isolates whole egg as the cause of the LDL rise
nodes.title: Because the LDL/ApoB rise was measured within-subject under blinded randomized crossover with an isocaloric egg-free control, the increase is causally attributable to egg intake rather than to diet or subject confounders.

nodes.label: A-2 - Mortality-without-events pattern in PHS points to residual confounding, not causal egg harm
nodes.title: The absence of an MI or stroke association alongside raised all-cause mortality is evidence against a cardiovascular-mediated causal harm of eggs in PHS; it does not by itself select residual confounding over a non-cardiovascular causal pathway.

nodes.label: A-3 - Attenuation on excluding early follow-up indicates reverse causation drives EPIC egg-IHD signal
nodes.title: The disappearance of the inverse egg-IHD association after excluding the first 4 years of follow-up is evidence that reverse causation contributes to the weak protective signal, and is consistent with a near-null true effect, but does not establish it.

nodes.label: A-4 - Significant dose-response trend strengthens causal reading of the CKB protective association
nodes.title: A monotone significant dose-response across egg-intake categories raises the plausibility that the CKB protective association is causal rather than a threshold confounding artifact, though it does not rule out graded confounding.

nodes.label: A-5 - Stable-but-unpredictable responder classes make the population-average LDL response misleading for subgroups
nodes.title: The population-average dietary-cholesterol response overstates harm for hypo-responders and understates it for hyperresponders

nodes.label: A-6 - Square-root dose-response implies each additional egg's serum-cholesterol effect shrinks at high baseline intake
nodes.title: Because the dose-response is concave (square-root), the marginal serum-cholesterol effect of an extra increment of dietary cholesterol falls as baseline intake rises, so linear extrapolation overstates incremental harm at habitual high intakes

nodes.label: H-1 - Habitual whole-egg intake raises atherogenic LDL-ApoB and thereby CVD risk
nodes.title: Habitual whole-egg consumption raises atherogenic LDL-C/ApoB and thereby increases cardiovascular risk.

nodes.label: H-10 - Egg intake has no material effect on blood lipids, CVD, or mortality across diverse global populations
nodes.title: Habitual egg intake is neutral for blood lipids, cardiovascular events, and mortality across low-, middle-, and high-income populations.

nodes.label: H-11 - Egg's effect on atherogenic blood lipids is characterized in some way not listed above
nodes.title: The effect of habitual egg intake on atherogenic blood lipids is characterized in some way not listed above (e.g., no measurable effect at all, or an effect operating mainly through non-ApoB pathways such as HDL or LDL particle size).

nodes.label: H-12 - Egg's net effect on hard cardiovascular and mortality endpoints is not a single population-wide direction
nodes.title: Egg intake's net effect on hard cardiovascular / mortality endpoints is not a single population-wide direction (e.g., it is non-monotonic or J-shaped - protective at moderate intake but harmful at high intake), so none of the listed single-direction answers holds.

nodes.label: H-13 - Egg's effect is essentially homogeneous across people with no stable protected subgroup or responder class
nodes.title: Egg intake's effect is essentially homogeneous across people - no stable protected subgroup (e.g. diabetics) or intrinsic responder class of the kinds listed above exists.

nodes.label: H-2 - Moderate egg intake around one per day is protective against CVD
nodes.title: Moderate egg intake (~1 egg/day) is protective against cardiovascular disease relative to rare consumption, at least in the Chinese population.

nodes.label: H-3 - High egg intake increases all-cause mortality, especially in diabetics
nodes.title: High egg intake (>=7/week) increases all-cause mortality, with a substantially larger effect among people with diabetes.

nodes.label: H-4 - Egg intake is essentially neutral for ischemic heart disease
nodes.title: Egg intake is essentially neutral for ischemic heart disease; the weak inverse association reflects reverse causation rather than a true protective effect.

nodes.label: H-5 - Poor bioavailability of egg cholesterol explains its weak effect on plasma cholesterol
nodes.title: Poor intestinal absorption of egg-matrix cholesterol explains why whole-egg feeding raises plasma cholesterol less than purified-cholesterol feeding would predict.

nodes.label: H-6 - Two eggs-day does not worsen cardiovascular risk factors in people with type 2 diabetes or prediabetes
nodes.title: Habitual intake of ~2 eggs/day does not adversely affect blood lipids or other cardiovascular risk factors in people with prediabetes or type 2 diabetes.

nodes.label: H-7 - A stable, lifestyle-independent subset of hypo-responders exists for whom dietary cholesterol raises serum cholesterol little
nodes.title: Individuals partition into stable hypo- vs hyperresponder classes whose dietary-cholesterol sensitivity is an intrinsic trait not predictable from lifestyle.

nodes.label: H-8 - Dietary cholesterol's effect on serum cholesterol is real but saturating, small at high habitual intakes
nodes.title: The dietary-cholesterol-to-serum-cholesterol effect is genuine but saturates, so incremental intake at already-high baseline raises serum cholesterol only modestly.

nodes.label: H-9 - Moderate habitual egg intake is not associated with increased CVD risk in generally healthy Western adults
nodes.title: Habitual egg intake up to ~1/day carries no material excess CVD risk in general adult populations.

nodes.label: Analysis of HC-1 - Magnitude and mechanism of egg's effect on atherogenic blood lipids
nodes.title: Analysis of HC-1 - Magnitude and mechanism of egg's effect on atherogenic blood lipids

nodes.title: Analysis of HC-2 - Net direction of egg's effect on hard cardiovascular and mortality endpoints

nodes.label: Analysis of HC-3 - Whether egg's effect varies by subgroup or individual responder type
nodes.title: Analysis of HC-3 - Whether egg's effect varies by subgroup or individual responder type

nodes.label: HC-1 - Magnitude and mechanism of egg's effect on atherogenic blood lipids
nodes.title: HC-1 - Magnitude and mechanism of egg's effect on atherogenic blood lipids

nodes.label: HC-2 - Net direction of egg's effect on hard cardiovascular and mortality endpoints
nodes.title: HC-2 - Net direction of egg's effect on hard cardiovascular and mortality endpoints

nodes.label: HC-3 - Whether egg's effect varies by subgroup or individual responder type
nodes.title: HC-3 - Whether egg's effect varies by subgroup or individual responder type

nodes.label: Main question
nodes.title: Is habitual egg consumption net beneficial, harmful, or neutral for human health — overall, for whom, and at what level of intake?

nodes.label: O-1 - One egg per day for 3 weeks raised LDL-C 12 percent and ApoB 9 percent in lacto-vegetarians
nodes.title: In a blinded randomized crossover (N=17 lacto-vegetarian students), adding one extra-large egg/day for 3 weeks (dietary cholesterol 97 to 418 mg/day) raised LDL-C by 12% and ApoB by 9% versus the egg-free period.

nodes.label: O-10 - EPIC egg-IHD inverse association became nonsignificant after excluding first 4 years
nodes.title: The EPIC egg-IHD inverse association became nonsignificant after excluding the first 4 years of follow-up (a reverse-causation sensitivity check).

nodes.label: O-11 - Cholesterol from whole eggs is poorly absorbed
nodes.title: In randomized crossover feeding, dietary cholesterol delivered as whole eggs was poorly absorbed in healthy men and women.

nodes.label: O-12 - Acute whole-egg meal did not raise plasma total cholesterol
nodes.title: An acute whole-egg meal did not raise plasma total cholesterol concentration in the crossover study.

nodes.label: O-13 - High-egg vs low-egg diet showed no between-group difference in blood lipids over 3 months in prediabetes-T2D (DIABEGG RCT)
nodes.title: In a 3-month parallel RCT (n=140 overweight adults with prediabetes/T2D), a high-egg diet (2 eggs/day, 6 d/wk) vs a low-egg diet (<2 eggs/wk) produced no significant between-group difference in LDL-C, HDL-C, total cholesterol, or triglycerides.

nodes.label: O-16 - Serum-cholesterol response to dietary cholesterol is consistent within individuals across repeated trials
nodes.title: Across 32 volunteers each completing 3 alternating low-/high-cholesterol feeding trials, individual serum-cholesterol responsiveness was reproducible, allowing stable classification into consistent hypo- and hyperresponders.

nodes.label: O-17 - Cholesterol responsiveness correlates with HDL2-total-cholesterol rise but not with habitual intake or body weight
nodes.title: Responsiveness correlated positively with the HDL2-cholesterol and total-cholesterol rise on the high-cholesterol diet, but showed no correlation with habitual cholesterol intake or body weight.

nodes.label: O-18 - Serum total cholesterol rises with dietary cholesterol (50-1450 mg-d) with diminishing returns, fit by a square-root dose-response
nodes.title: In 227 men across 19 controlled metabolic-ward comparisons with all other diet variables held constant, serum total cholesterol increased with dietary cholesterol dose (50-1450 mg/d) but with strongly diminishing returns, best fit by a square-root rather than linear term.

nodes.label: O-19 - Up to at least 1 egg-day was not associated with incident CVD in pooled NHS-NHSII-HPFS cohorts
nodes.title: In the pooled Harvard cohorts (n=215,618; 5.5M person-years; 14,806 incident CVD events), consumption of at least 1 egg/day vs <1/month was not associated with incident CVD after updated confounder adjustment (HR 0.93, confidence interval crossing 1).

nodes.label: O-2 - HDL, apolipoproteins A, VLDL-C and triglycerides unchanged during egg feeding
nodes.title: During the same egg-feeding period HDL-C, ApoA-I, ApoA-II, VLDL-C and total triglycerides did not change significantly, so the lipid effect was confined to LDL/ApoB.

nodes.label: O-20 - Egg intake was not significantly associated with blood lipids, mortality, or major CVD in the PURE 21-country cohort
nodes.title: In PURE (146,011 participants across 21 countries; 14,700 composite events), egg intake showed no significant association with blood lipids, all-cause mortality, or major CVD in the primary analyses.

nodes.label: O-21 - A higher-intake-lower-MI signal appeared in PURE alone but was not replicated in ONTARGET-TRANSCEND
nodes.title: A signal of higher egg intake associated with lower myocardial-infarction risk appeared in PURE alone and was not replicated in the ONTARGET/TRANSCEND patient cohorts, so the authors flag it as likely spurious.

nodes.label: O-4 - Daily egg intake associated with lower CVD, IHD and hemorrhagic stroke risk with dose-response in CKB
nodes.title: In the China Kadoorie Biobank (461,213 adults, median 8.9 y, 83,977 CVD cases), daily egg consumers (~0.76 egg/day) had lower risk than non/rare consumers: HR 0.89 CVD, 0.88 IHD, 0.86 major coronary events, 0.74 hemorrhagic stroke, 0.90 ischemic stroke, with a significant dose-response trend.

nodes.label: O-5 - Daily egg consumption associated with 18 percent lower CVD mortality in CKB
nodes.title: In the same CKB cohort daily egg consumption was associated with 18% lower CVD mortality (HR ~0.82) versus non/rare consumption.

nodes.label: O-6 - Egg intake not associated with incident MI or stroke in Physicians Health Study
nodes.title: In the Physicians' Health Study (21,327 male physicians, ~20 y, 1550 MIs, 1342 strokes) egg consumption was not associated with incident myocardial infarction or stroke.

nodes.label: O-7 - All-cause mortality rose with egg intake in PHS, HR 1.23 for 7 plus per week
nodes.title: In the PHS all-cause mortality rose with egg intake: HR 1.23 for >=7 eggs/week versus <1/week (5169 deaths), P-trend<0.0001.

nodes.label: O-8 - Egg-mortality association about twice as strong in diabetic PHS subgroup, HR 2.01
nodes.title: The egg-mortality association was roughly twice as strong among diabetic physicians (HR 2.01 highest vs lowest intake category, P-interaction=0.09, exploratory).

nodes.label: O-9 - Egg intake modestly inversely associated with IHD in EPIC, HR 0.93 per 20 g per day
nodes.title: In EPIC (409,885 adults, 9 countries, mean 12.6 y, 7198 incident MI/fatal-IHD cases) egg intake was modestly inversely associated with IHD, HR 0.93 per 20 g/day increment.

nodes.label: O-15 - A large egg contains roughly 185-200 mg of dietary cholesterol, almost all in the yolk
nodes.title: One large chicken egg supplies approximately 185-200 mg of dietary cholesterol, essentially all of it in the yolk.

nodes.label: O-14 - High-egg diet increased reported satiety vs low-egg diet in the DIABEGG RCT
nodes.title: Participants on the high-egg (2 eggs/day) arm reported greater satiety than those on the low-egg arm over the 3-month weight-maintenance period.

nodes.label: O-3 - A single egg yolk contains roughly 200-300 mg of dietary cholesterol
nodes.title: A single egg yolk (whole large egg ~185-300 mg) contains on the order of 200-300 mg of dietary cholesterol, almost all in the yolk (here one extra-large egg raised intake from 97 to 418 mg/day).
